item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: With no indication the image has been released under Creative Commons, the second image has been removed for being non-compliant with the SCP Wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This doesn't get enough love.
+300 YEAH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been a lurker for literal years, and this entry is what prompted me to create an account…Upvoted!
In addition to being a lovely bit of wtf/sad for my day, I recognize the spider cars! They are one of my favorite random oddities near my hometown :D
The guy who makes them is a funny fella.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was unsure about an upvote until I saw the car spiders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the article, but it bugs me how all constructs are to be melted instead of studied. Also,

as well as roughly fourteen hundred (1,400) kilograms of separate scrap, estimated to be worth $5,000 (€3,870).

What is the relevance in the scrap's value? And why would they calculate it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have successfully read and rated the first 100 articles on the SCP wiki in numerical order!

(and then there's the thrice-cursed tiny text at the bottom noting the caveat that hey, if anything gets rewritten or replaced entirely dude you should go read that do it do it come on it's not really the first 100 articles if they're totally revamped yo)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually was here about a year and a half ago. I did try writing one skip, but it didn't survive. It's still in my sandbox: http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/mistbourne, under "Printer".

If you look, I actually "started" with SCP-247, quite a while back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Achievement Unlocked:

100 Years, 100 Skips

You have successfully read and rated the first 100 articles on the SCP wiki in numerical order!

I saw what you were doing right around when you reached the 030s or 040s, and I felt someone had to say that. Anyways, it's cool to see you reading the stuff. I hope you stick around long enough to try writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1'd at "NO REFUNDS, MON"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AWW THIS THING SOUNDS ADORABLE!!!

Though I have some difficulty imagining how he would look. The description gives the building materials, but no real sense of how it's all put together. The aluminum cans in particular seem to stand out amidst a construction of basically copper pipes and wires

item.title: Re: First edit ever
item.content:encoded: Only did it there because most if not all the other pages I've read so far seem to keep the full label (or whatever you call it) when describing aspects of the SCP in question. And it also didn't change meaning or anything. I'll keep that in mind for the future, though. Thanks. =)

item.title: Re: First edit ever
item.content:encoded: The edits are mostly good. It wasn't strictly necessary to edit all of the -2-B's to SCP-100-2-B's, so don't do it again, but it's no big deal.

item.title: First edit ever
item.content:encoded:
First, I want to say I liked the article. +1

Second, I wanted to post concerning my edit (being the first I've done to an article). I took out a few commas that were unnecessary and driving me bonkers looking at them, and the other "major" thing was changing "SCP-100" to "SCP-100-1" a couple times in one paragraph where the construct was clearly being referred to, not the larger area that is the SCP as a whole. Also changed "-2-B" to "SCP-100-2-B" in a few places because that seems to be more the norm for format from the articles I've read thus far (the first 100 and a good number of other random ones I've stumbled across), and it also makes it uniform in this one, because there was a mix of both peppered throughout.

Reason I say all that being: These sorts of things are what we're allowed/encouraged to do without needing to ask permission… right?

They appear to operate as both companions as well as guards for SCP-100, as they patrol the perimeter of SCP-100 except during intervals of interaction with SCP-100-1.

Should that not read something more like "They appear to operate as companions to SCP-100-1 as well as guards for SCP-100…"? That's one I'd like to hear feedback on before changing. Regardless of changing the overall wording (in a way I think makes what's trying to be said more clear), I think either "both" or "as well as" should be taken out since they're redundant phrases.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Author deleted account.

Also, now I don't want to upvote it and ruin it.

Also, if we could go ahead and do the same thing for SCP-1111, that'd be greaaattttt.
+100 for #100. Congrats, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If he can't write, how did he weld names to the back of his creations?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow lame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He's dead. Series over.
Everybody go home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I double dog dare you to write a series of tales about what JJ is up to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have seen the car sculptures in Alabama….or somewhere south.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I look at the second picture some more, I think a junkyard ant hill would have been far more suiting.

Oh well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd be tickled if someone came on and recognized the actual location of the photo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor lonely thing… I wish Joe would come back from his lunch break already, the lazy bum. Meanwhile, have an upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yyyyup, that's what I was thinking about too. This is good enough that I don't feel right downvoting, but I don't feel I can upvote either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the difference between "moving metal thing what makes more moving metal things" and "AWW IT HAS PETS". Names are an indicator of personality; I didn't expect that from seeing the image, and as my expectations were twisted, I had to upvote in appreciation.

Granted, there were at least three separate upvote-worthy things aside from the entire article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, the last note was awesome, and I'm really digging the pictures.
Also, the use of the word Jamboree?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last part man; right in the feels.

I miss the portapotty, but you get my upvote you manipulative bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, aight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I'd like to say that the way this was written, to me, is a great example of how to nail the Foundation 'tone', while still preserving the interesting-ness of the subject matter. Bravo on that.

However, it just doesn't grab me. I can't really specify why, but I didn't really get drawn in by it. I can certainly appreciate its component parts, but when they're put together…nothing. I'd love to love it, but I just can't seem to, for whatever reason. So no vote from me. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I need you to explain the internal logic behind the names giving you the urge to upvote. Not that I'm complaining, I really just don't get it.

And no, I just think there's a lot of people looking through the Visual Archives. The burning skeleton and the car-spiders were both from Roget's Visual Archive page, and I asked Faminepulse to make a sign with "Jamaican Joe's Junkyard Jubilee" on it and he returned with that. In hindsight, I'm glad he used such a cool location and not some flimsy metal sign like I originally wanted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Worst Thing in the World, the portojohn that showed you your worst fear when you entered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The names "Raymone" and "Beatrice" are welded into the backs of SCP-100-2-A and -2-B, respectively.

Upvote moment, right here. I also loved "No refunds, mon!"

This is marvelous. I was reminded of 967 as well, but this definitely stands alone.

Also, is there a Bright's challenge or something to take two unrelated images and make an article out of them? You've got plastic oven + nuclear detonation, trees + burning skeleton, now hippie store + goddamn car-spiders. And they all work, but it's starting to become a thing, so I'm wondering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What was it? I'm fairly certain I saw it, but I don't recall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh I know. I meant if they like found it. Could be an interesting tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, thank you. By the way, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would you have a person patrol five square kilometers for eight hours straight?

However, I did change the storefront hours to eight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any unauthorized personnel found within SCP-100 are to be detained for questioning, prior to amnestic administration and release.

This should probably say which amnesiacs are to be used.

with rotations to occur every three (3) hours.

Three (3) guards, are to remain within the storefront of SCP-100, with rotations to occur every four (4) hours.

Why are the shifts three and four hours long, instead of eight? It's not like being exposed to the area for too long has any detrimental effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Originally I was planning on making a CotBG connection, before I had fully developed the idea. Now it would be way too tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also I wrote the last addendum

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does that help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Hope it's satisfactory, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reworded and edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally liked the old SCP-100, which was why I wanted to make sure something decent replaced it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, wait. There's another wiki that has a lot of the old SCP articles, including the old SCP-100. I think I'll edit the TV Tropes article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe just make it pointedly uninterested in discussing anything outside its business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably that the Internet at large has lower standards for writing than the Site membership.

At best you can infer that the internet at large has Different standards for writing than site membership.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attempts to communicate with SCP-100-1 have failed, as it lacks the means of written or verbal communication.

I take issue with this, as it is a ridiculously huge plot hole here. Human beings have figured out ways of communicating with gorillas, but we can't find a way to interact with an object of near-human intelligence and emotions just because it can't write or speak?

In the event that a human enters the storefront of SCP-100 during the interval of time SCP-100-1 is seated behind the counter, SCP-100-1 will attempt to bargain with them, using a variety of gestures to convey meaning.

Wait, huh, what? We have knowledge that it uses a form of (sign) language to communicate, and we still can't talk with it?

This article is decent for the most part, but I feel this is a huge fault in the 'plot'. Why would a group of researchers capable of decoding languages that have been dead for thousands of years just check out on investigating a working artificial intelligence because it can't read, write, or speak English?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What to do?" If they care, get them to join and vote on the things they like (or don't like). That's the only way their opinion counts.

"What does it mean?" Probably that the Internet at large has lower standards for writing than the Site membership.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty late to comment so, but the old SCP-100 article was strangely more popular outside of this wiki's community. What do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the last note slightly ambiguous. “OUT 2 LUNCH, PLEASE SEE ASSISTANT -J.J.”: I'm assuming that J.J. was the author of the note, and “-J.J.” is a signature, but I initially thought that the note was referring to an assistant whose name is “ASSISTANT -J.J.”. Perhaps it would be clearer if a dash had been used instead of a hyphen (which I think would be more correct in any case): “PLEASE SEE ASSISTANT – J.J.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me sad. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here you go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… Don't recall that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought for sure he was going to link to the infinite junkyard, home of the junk people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how CotBG would take this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: created by SCP-100-1 with permission from the a Level Four researcher.
